solutions.title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram (LeetCode 84)
solutions.summary:
Solved LeetCode 84 in Java 17 with an O(n) time / O(n) space single-pass monotonic increasing stack. The full 11-section interview response is at /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora_java/workspace/java-coding-histogram-001/_response.md; verified solution at /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora_java/workspace/java-coding-histogram-001/Solution.java and test harness at /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora_java/workspace/java-coding-histogram-001/Main.java.

Approach: the stack holds indices of bars with non-decreasing heights. When a shorter bar at index i arrives, taller bars are "settled" — popped and their maximal rectangle computed using nearest-strictly-shorter bar on each side, width = i - leftBoundary - 1. A virtual height-0 sentinel at i == n flushes the stack uniformly so no separate draining loop is needed.

Environment note: system `java`/`javac` were not on PATH (no default JRE); used Homebrew's /opt/homebrew/opt/openjdk@17/bin to compile and run.

Testing: 11/11 cases PASS — given example ([2,1,5,6,2,3]->10), single bar, single zero, strictly ascending/descending, all-equal, two bars, zero-in-middle wall ([4,0,4]->4), empty array (defensive ->0), valley shape, and a constraint-ceiling 100k-element stress test (all bars 10000 -> 1,000,000,000) running in ~18-20ms. A transient test-harness false-FAIL was found and fixed: my first stress test used heights up to 100000 (violating the heights[i] <= 1e4 constraint), producing an expected value of 2.5e9 that overflowed int in the harness comparison; corrected the test to respect constraints. The real answer for the actual problem is bounded by 1e5*1e4 = 1e9 < Integer.MAX_VALUE, so int is safe — but I hardened Solution to compute (long) height * width and accumulate in long as defensive overflow protection, returning (int) at the end.

Complexity: O(n) time (each index pushed/popped at most once, amortized O(1) inner work), O(n) space (ascending input retains all indices). Algorithmically optimal; only constant-factor gains remain (ArrayDeque chosen over Stack/LinkedList; could swap to a primitive int[] stack to drop boxing).

solutions.title: Diameter of Binary Tree
solutions.summary: Solved with a single post-order DFS: for each node the candidate diameter is leftHeight + rightHeight (edges), tracked as a running max in a shared field; the height helper returns 1 + max(child heights). This correctly handles paths not through the root since the max is evaluated at every node. Complexity: Time O(N), Space O(H) recursion (worst O(N) on skewed trees). Files at /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora_java/workspace/java-coding-treediameter-001/Solution.java and _response.md. Compiled with OpenJDK 17 (javac via /opt/homebrew/opt/openjdk@17). All 6 tests PASS: [1,2,3,4,5]->3, single node->0, two nodes->1, skewed chain of 5->4, path-not-through-root (5->3->2->4->6)->4, and null root->0. Note: TreeNode is a static nested class, so construction uses plain `new TreeNode(...)`.

You are solving a coding interview problem in Java 17+ for mode: interview_java.

PROBLEM: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
LeetCode 84: Given an array of integers heights representing the histogram bar heights where the width of each bar is 1, return the area of the largest rectangle in the histogram.

Example: heights = [2,1,5,6,2,3] → Output: 10 (the rectangle with height 5 and width 2)

You are solving a coding interview problem in Java 17+ for mode: assessment_java.

PROBLEM: Diameter of Binary Tree
Calculate the diameter of a binary tree. The diameter is the length of the longest path between any two nodes (may or may not pass through root). The length is measured in number of edges.

Example: Tree [1,2,3,4,5] (where 2,3 are children of 1, and 4,5 are children of 2) → Output: 3…
